coredata.dc:title: Efficient synthesis of high-purity carbon dots via self-polymerization driven bottom-up growth for high-selectivity sensing and high-efficiency separation
coredata.dc:creator: Yuan, Long
Yan, Meiting
Tao, Xueping
Yang, Xiao
Long, Wenwen
Xia, Wenli
Liao, Hongyan
Gong, Yuanmin
Zhang, Kailian
Xia, Zhining
Fu, Qifeng
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive interest due to their exceptional optical properties and facile synthesis processes. However, achieving high-efficiency synthesis of high-purity CDs with high yield remains an unresolved critical issue. Herein, we report a facile and versatile self-polymerization-driven bottom-up growth strategy for efficient fabrication of high-purity CDs. As a proof-of-concept demonstration, dopamine and its four analogues were used as the self-polymerizable precursors for self-driven synthesis of fluorescent CDs with high-purity. By employing a mixed-solvent system and rationally regulating periodate oxidation process, the highly controllable self-polymerization processes and efficient solvothermal-assisted production of polydopamine and polyphenols derived CDs were readily achieved. Further integrating high molecular weight membrane dialysis, we successfully obtained the highly purified polydopamine-derived CDs with high yield of 21.1 % and quantum yield of 3.6 %, confirmed by nuclear magnetic resonance, capillary electrophoresis and other types of high-resolution characterization techniques. The formation and luminescence mechanisms of the self-polymerized CDs were also elucidated through comparative analysis of their morphological features and elemental compositions. We also demonstrated the broad universality of this self-polymerization driven bottom-up strategy by utilizing by employing four other phenolic compounds, including l-dopa, gallic acid, catechol, and pyrogallol, as readily accessible precursors for high-yield synthesizing highly purified fluorescent CDs. Moreover, the enormous application potential of the high-purity CDs for high-selectivity sensing and high-efficiency electrochromatographic separation was thoroughly demonstrated.
coredata.dcterms:subject: High-purity carbon dots
Self-polymerization driven growth
Rigorous purification
High-selectivity sensing
High-efficiency separation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic of the rational synthesis, rigorous purification, validation and application of high-purity PDA-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Yields and QYs of PDA-derived a) crude solvothermal products and b) 15 kDa retentates. c) The fluorescence images before and after 15 kDa membrane dialysis of PDA-derived solvothermal products. d-f) Yields and QYs of SP/EtOH-mediated 15 kDa retentates prepared using different contents of EtOH, DA and SP. TEM/HR-TEM images of g) SP-PDA-CDs, h) Tris-PDA-CDs, i) SE-PDA-CDs and j) TE-PDA-CDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
a-d) XPS survey and high-resolution C 1s and N 1s spectra of SP-PDA-CDs, Tris-PDA-CDs, SE-PDA-CDs, and TE-PDA-CDs, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) Yields and QYs of SE-PDA-CDs products with different purification level. b) QYs of the 1, 1–5, and 5–15 kDa dialysates of SE-PDA-CDs products. c-f) Fluorescence spectra of unpurified, 1 kDa, 5 kDa and 15 kDa retentates of SE-PDA-CDs products upon excitation at different wavelengths. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a) UV–vis, b) FTIR, c) Raman, e) 1H NMR spectra and d) electropherograms of unpurified, 1 kDa, 5 kDa, 15 kDa retentates and 15 kDa dialysate of SE-PDA-CDs products. Different colored curves represent the corresponding samples: black for crude SE-PDA-CDs, red for 1 kDa retentate, green for 5 kDa retentate, blue for 15 kDa retentate, and cyan for 15 kDa dialysate. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

a-d) Fluorescence intensities of SE-PDA-CDs products with different purification level after the addition of different analytes. e) The linear relationship between the fluorescent quenching value ΔF of SE-PDA-CDs products with different purification level and TC content ranged from 0.5 to 40 μM. f) EOF mobilities of bare column, unpurified CDs@capillary, and 15 kDa retentate@capillary at different buffer pH values. Conditions: background electrolyte, 50.0 mM phosphate buffer (pH 5.0–9.0); voltage, 25 kV. Electropherograms of g) nucleosides (1. cytidine, 2. uridine, 3. deoxyadenosine) and h) alkaloids (1. theobromine, 2. theophylline, 3. xanthine) on the bare column, unpurified CDs@capillary, and 15 kDa retentate@capillary. Conditions: 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 8.0), 3 % ACN (v/v), 25 kV for g); 50 mM phosphate buffer (pH 6.5), 5 % ACN (v/v), 28 kV for h). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Efficient synthesis of high-purity carbon dots via self-polymerization driven bottom-up growth for high-selectivity sensing and high-efficiency separation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, 646000, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmacy, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, 646000, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Humanities and Management Science, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, 646000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Humanities and Management Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Humanities and Management Science, Southwest Medical University, Luzhou, 646000, China.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Pharmaceutical Sciences, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 401331, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted extensive interest due to their exceptional optical properties and facile synthesis processes. However, achieving high-efficiency synthesis of high-purity CDs with high yield remains an unresolved critical issue. Herein, we report a facile and versatile self-polymerization-driven bottom-up growth strategy for efficient fabrication of high-purity CDs. As a proof-of-concept demonstration, dopamine and its four analogues were used as the self-polymerizable precursors for self-driven synthesis of fluorescent CDs with high-purity. By employing a mixed-solvent system and rationally regulating periodate oxidation process, the highly controllable self-polymerization processes and efficient solvothermal-assisted production of polydopamine and polyphenols derived CDs were readily achieved. Further integrating high molecular weight membrane dialysis, we successfully obtained the highly purified polydopamine-derived CDs with high yield of 21.1 % and quantum yield of 3.6 %, confirmed by nuclear magnetic resonance, capillary electrophoresis and other types of high-resolution characterization techniques. The formation and luminescence mechanisms of the self-polymerized CDs were also elucidated through comparative analysis of their morphological features and elemental compositions. We also demonstrated the broad universality of this self-polymerization driven bottom-up strategy by utilizing by employing four other phenolic compounds, including l-dopa, gallic acid, catechol, and pyrogallol, as readily accessible precursors for high-yield synthesizing highly purified fluorescent CDs. Moreover, the enormous application potential of the high-purity CDs for high-selectivity sensing and high-efficiency electrochromatographic separation was thoroughly demonstrated.

A novel self-polymerization driven solvothermal technique has been proposed for the efficient synthesis of highly purified fluorescent CDs with high yield through rational regulation of the polymerization and carbonization pathways of DA and other self-polymerizable precursors. The high purity was achieved through rigorous purification methods and confirmed by multiple high-resolution characterization techniques. The high-purity CDs exhibit significant importance in enhancing sensing selectivity and improving chromatographic separation efficiency, highlighting their great potential for applications in precise detection and advanced fabrication of separation media.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: As a type of novel fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have garnered significant interest due to their exceptional luminescent properties and simple synthesis process [1,2]. CDs are typically synthesized through top-down or bottom-up approaches, with the latter being more popular due to its versatility and practicality [3–5]. In particular, solvothermal or hydrothermal treatment of small molecules, polymer or biomass precursors has become a highly important bottom-up method for preparing these nanoparticles [4,6–8]. However, the high-temperature treatment of precursors can lead to uncontrolled complex formation processes (e.g., dehydration, condensation, cross-linking, carbonization, etc.) and diverse reaction pathways [9], resulting in the massive formation of undesired fluorescent small molecular byproducts and their assembled supramolecular clusters alongside CDs [10,11]. The presence of these impurity species may predominantly contribute to the observed strong fluorescence properties of the crude CD products, which can potentially result in misleading and inconsistent conclusions regarding the structure and fluorescent characteristics of the investigated “CDs” [12,13]. For instance, the observed fluorescence properties of some representative CDs have been identified as primarily caused by molecular fluorophores [14–16]. In this context, the development of highly controlled methods for efficiently fabricating high-purity CDs with high yield is challenging yet crucial.

Despite the attention given to the purity issue of CDs by some researchers, inadequate or even the absence of purification steps still prevails in the literature. Consequently, prolonged debates persist regarding the fundamental structure and fluorescence properties of CDs, thereby posing significant obstacles to their practical applications. Membrane dialysis has been a commonly employed purification technique for CDs [17], typically utilizing membranes with a molecular weight cutoff (MWCO) below 5 kDa [18]. However, previous study have shown that low molecular weight membrane dialysis is insufficient in fully removing fluorescent by-products (especially polymeric, supramolecular, or aggregated species) generated during the synthesis of CDs [19]. Alternatively, high molecular weight membrane dialysis treatment has the potential to significantly increase the purity of certain typical “strongly emitting” CDs systems, but at the expense of extremely low fluorescence quantum yields (QYs) and yields, possibly due to aforementioned diverse bottom-up reaction routes. Therefore, in addition to improving the controllability of reaction pathways, the use of high-efficiency purification techniques such as high molecular weight membrane dialysis is an important prerequisite for obtaining high-purity CDs.

Characterization techniques such as transmission electron microscopy (TEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) have conventionally been employed to analyze and confirm the structure of CDs in previous studies [20,21]. However, these techniques are inadequate in discerning the presence of molecular species in CD samples, thus being one of the significant reasons for the widespread purity issue. For example, although TEM is one of the most commonly used characterization method of CDs, the limited resolution makes it difficult to observe small molecular impurities and distinguish between CDs and aggregates of organic molecules [22]. Therefore, it is necessary to introduce higher resolution characterization techniques and high-efficiency separation methods to confirm the purification level of CD products. Notably, recent studies have highlighted the efficacy of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) and capillary electrophoresis (CE) techniques for the identification and separation of molecular species in CD samples [23–26].

Polydopamine (PDA) is a well-known mussel-mimicking functional material that possesses many attractive properties, such as versatile adhesion capability and extremely mild and efficient self-polymerization reaction processes [27–29]. Typically, dopamine (DA) serves as a precursor building block, readily self-polymerizes into PDA coatings on substrates or PDA nanoparticles in solutions under alkaline or oxidant-induced conditions [30]. Although the exact self-polymerization mechanism of PDA is still debated, there is a general consensus that the self-assembly and covalent polymerization of 5,6-dihydroxyindole (DHI) and its cyclized derivatives play a vital role in the reaction process of DA [31–33], resulting in an interconnected layered structure of PDA similar to natural eumelanin and well-organized graphite-like nanostructure of carbonized PDA nanoparticles [28,34]. Moreover, the controllability and consistency of the polymerization pathways of DA can be further improved through mixed-solvent-mediated, boronic acid derivatives co-polymerization and other strategies [33,35,36], leading to highly uniform morphology and structure of PDA. Therefore, the highly spontaneous and controllable PDA-based self-polymerization systems with uniform homogeneity hold promising prospects for the bottom-up synthesis of high-purity CDs. Previous studies have utilized DA as a precursor for the bottom-up synthesis of fluorescent CDs [37,38]. However, these traditional bottom-up synthesis methods, which rely solely on hydrothermal or neutralization heat to drive the growth of DA-derived CDs, still suffer from issues such as complex formation processes, poor controllability over the reaction pathway, and incomplete purification, leading to low conversion efficiency and questionable purity of the resulting CDs.

Herein, inspired by the high-efficiency self-polymerization properties of DA and graphite-like nanostructure of carbonized PDA, we present a facile and versatile self-polymerization driven solvothermal method for highly-efficient bottom-up fabricating high-purity CDs (Fig. 1 ). For a proof-of-concept demonstration, DA were used as the model self-polymerizable precursor for self-driven synthesis of fluorescent CDs with high-purity. The controllability of the self-polymerization and subsequent solvothermal carbonization processes of PDA was found to be significantly improved using the mixed solvent-mediated periodate oxidation strategy, thereby contributing greatly to the enhancement of conversion efficiency of CDs. The crude solvothermal product was further rigorously purified by dialysis with a membrane MWCO of 15 kDa, resulting in successful preparation of highly purified PDA derived CDs (PDA-CDs) with high yield. Through comparative analysis of the morphological features and elemental compositions of PDA-CDs synthesized with different reaction systems, we revealed their possible formation and luminescence mechanisms. The high purity of PDA-CDs was confirmed through various characterization techniques such as UV–vis absorption spectroscopy, Raman spectroscopy, NMR, mass spectrometry (MS) and CE separation. We also demonstrated the broad universality of this self-polymerization driven bottom-up strategy by utilizing by employing four other phenolic compounds, including l-dopa, gallic acid, catechol, and pyrogallol, as readily accessible precursors for efficiently synthesizing highly purified fluorescent CDs. Furthermore, a systematic investigation was conducted to evaluate the fluorescence sensing and electrochromatographic separation performance of PDA-CDs with different degrees of purification, which thoroughly demonstrates that high purity is an essential prerequisite to ensure high selectivity and high efficiency in sensing and chromatographic separation applications of CDs. By exploiting the exceptional sensing specificity of highly purified PDA-CDs, ultra-fast and accurate quantification of trace amounts of tetracycline in complex real samples was successfully accomplished.

We initially conducted an intensive study on three representative CDs derived from commonly used precursors, including citric acid/ethylenediamine (CA/EDA) [39], o-phenylenediamine (OPD) [40], and oatmeal biomass (OM) [41]. The unpurified CDs showed high yields and QYs (Fig. S1), corresponding to previous findings. However, as the MWCO of the dialysis membrane increased from 1 kDa to 15 kDa, both the color and fluorescence intensity of the retentates significantly decreased under sunlight and 365 nm UV lamp. Specifically, after 15 kDa membrane dialysis, the purified CDs had yields and QYs lower than 1 %. It has been demonstrated that the utilization of low molecular weight membranes for dialysis encounters challenge in completely eliminating byproducts formed during the CD formation process [19]. Therefore, these findings illustrate the potential issues and misconceptions associated with inadequate purification protocols in previously reported bottom-up synthesis methods of CDs.

Subsequently, the potential utilization of DA as a self-polymerizable precursor for fabricating high-purity PDA-derived CDs (PDA-CDs) is further investigated. The classic Tris buffer-based PDA reaction system was used for hydrothermal carbonization treatment of DA, resulting in the massive formation of insoluble PDA-derived hydrochars (PDA-HCs) and a small quantity of Tris-induced PDA-CDs (Tris-PDA-CDs) in the post-reaction solution. Accordingly, the yield and QY of the supernatant crude product are also not prominent (Fig. 2 a). After 15 kDa membrane dialysis for 7 d, the yield of the retentates markedly increased to nearly 5 % (Fig. 2b), while also exhibiting a relatively limited fluorescence property (QY < 1 %). These results suggest the potential of self-polymerizable precursors for synthesizing high-purity CDs. The relatively limited yield of the retentates may be attributed to the poor controllability of DA auto-oxidation process. Specifically, a large proportion of DA molecules can be rapidly and uncontrollably auto-oxidized under hydrothermal conditions, resulting in the formation of large-sized PDA nanoparticles, which eventually convert to PDA-HCs. Apart from converting to Tris-PDA-CDs, the residual DA molecules can also spontaneously transform into chemically distinct oligomers and stacked oligomer fractions through various self-polymerization mechanisms and reaction pathways [42,43], which can be easily removed by the high molecular weight membrane dialysis. As for another representative PDA reaction system, i.e., sodium periodate (SP)-induced oxidation of DA in acidic media [44], the precipitation after hydrothermal treatment was minimal and the yield of the pale-yellow supernatant crude product was 82.9 %. However, after 15 kDa membrane dialysis for 7 d, the yield of the SP-induced PDA-CDs (SP-PDA-CDs) was less than 2 %, with a very weak fluorescence intensity (Fig. 2b). These phenomena indicate that the polymerization efficiency of DA is markedly inhibited in the presence of SP, which can be attributed to the periodate-induced excessive carboxylation of the key cyclized intermediates for the formation of PDA [33,44]. Consequently, the majority of products in SP-induced DA hydrothermal process are low-molecular-weight byproducts. Similarly, if the reaction system is replaced with anhydrous ethanol or the ultrapure water that was previously used in the earlier study [37], which were adverse to the self-polymerization reaction of DA [35], very low yields and QYs of the corresponding 15 kDa retentates can also be observed (Tables S1 and S2).

Alternatively, the possible influence of our previously reported mixed-solvent-mediated PDA reaction system on the hydrothermal treatment of DA was further investigated. Upon the addition of 60 % ethanol (EtOH), the post-reaction solutions of both Tris buffer-based and SP-induced systems exhibited darker hues and significantly enhanced fluorescence properties, accompanied by an apparent reduction in precipitates. The yields and QYs of the 15 kDa retentates were also improved to varying degrees (Fig. 2a and b). Especially, both the yield (21.1 %) and QY (3.6 %) of SP-induced retentates significantly increased in the presence of 60 % EtOH (Fig. 2b and c). These phenomena indicate that introducing mixed-solvent significantly improved the reaction efficiency and controllability of SP-induced polymerization of DA, which can be ascribed to the effectively inhibited uncontrolled autoxidation and SP-induced excessive carboxylation processes as well as significantly facilitated critical reaction pathway involved in the formation of the DHI-derived cyclized moieties in the mixed-solvent system [33,45]. Correspondingly, the formation of PDA-HCs due to excessive oxidative polymerization as well as the oligomer fractions generated from diverse reaction pathways were both effectively suppressed.

Given the superior characteristics of SP/EtOH-mediated 15 kDa retentates, we conducted a detailed investigation on the influence of reaction parameters on their generation efficiency and fluorescence properties. With the increase in EtOH concentration, the color of the crude product solution deepens gradually and the yields of the 15 kDa retentates significantly increased (Fig. 2d), further verifying the role of the mixed-solvent system on enhancing the polymerization efficiency of DA. Excessive elevation of the DA and SP concentrations results in more formation of PDA-HCs in the crude product, along with a significant reduction in the yields and QYs of the retentates (Fig. 2e and f). These findings indicate that the conversion efficiency and fluorescence performance of PDA-derived “CDs” can be easily regulated by the contents of DA precursor and oxidant.

From these above results, there are at least three categories of heterogeneous populations with varying sizes present in the solvothermal products of DA precursor: (1) a population of low-molecular-weight species that significantly contribute to the observed fluorescence; (2) a population of PDA-derived fluorescent “CDs” that are retained by a 15 kDa membrane dialysis; (3) a population of insoluble PDA-HCs with larger size and poor fluorescent properties. As listed in Table S3, the other two competitive reaction pathways of DA are unfavorable for efficient synthesis of PDA-CDs. Fortunately, by introducing a mixed-solvent-mediated system and rational regulation of SP-induced reaction parameters, we can easily achieve the controllable polymerization of PDA and high-efficiency synthesis of highly fluorescent PDA-CDs with minimal insoluble PDA-HCs and low-molecular-weight byproducts during hydrothermal processing.

To ascertain the possible presence of PDA-CDs in the purified products obtained from the above four representative solvothermal-assisted PDA reaction systems, their morphologies were assessed using TEM and AFM. All the four 15 kDa retentates contain well-defined nanoparticles with an average size about 1.8–2.7 nm (Fig. S2). High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image shows that these particles are crystalline with a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm (Fig. 2g–j), attributing to the (100) facet of graphite. Interestingly, introducing mixed solvents resulted in a narrower size distribution and a smaller size of PDA-CDs. Compared with Tris/EtOH-mediated CDs (TE-PDA-CDs), the smaller particle size and more uniform morphology can be observed in the SP/EtOH-mediated CDs (SE-PDA-CDs) sample, which may be attributed to the high controllability of SP-mediated oxidation process of DA in mixed-solvent system [33]. The AFM images of SE-PDA-CDs indicates that their heights are around 1.5–3 nm (Fig. S3), corresponding to 2–4 graphene layers. Furthermore, the scanning electron microscope characterization results of PDA-HCs reveal that all the sediment particles exhibit quasi-spherical morphology, with the similar order of average size: Tris-PDA-HCs > TE-PDA-HCs > SE-PDA-HCs (Fig. S4). These findings further validate the mixed-solvent systems can effectively suppress uncontrolled oxidation processes of catechol/quinone units, contributing to the controlled growth of PDA with smaller particle sizes.

XPS spectroscopy was used to analyze the composition of as-prepared PDA-CDs (Fig. 3 and Table S4). Compared with PDA-NPs prepared at room temperature under similar conditions (Table S5), the corresponding PDA-CDs possess a higher carbon content, confirming that the hydrothermal products were effectively carbonized. As the ethanol content in the mixed solvent system increases, the O/C ratio and the proportion of CO bonds (corresponding to the carbonyl/carboxyl groups) in SE-PDA-NPs gradually decrease (Fig. S5). Similarly, the presence of EtOH led to higher carbon content and significantly lower O/C ratios in both SE-PDA-CDs and TE-PDA-CDs (Table S4). These phenomena can be ascribed to the facts that mixed-solvent system can effectively suppress the oxidative cleavage and excessive carboxylation of quinone units in PDA [33]. Furthermore, the higher proportions of pyrrolic N and CC bonds (corresponding to the isolated sp2 domains within the hybridized carbon networks) of PDA-CDs were observed after the introduction of EtOH, especially SE-PDA-CDs (Fig. 3c). These results indicate that mixed solvent can effectively promote the formation of cyclized moieties and graphite microstructures in PDA-CDs. Considering the dominant role of isolated sp2 clusters in the fluorescence emission of graphitized CDs [46,47], it is reasonable that SE-PDA-CDs exhibit the highest QY, as they possess the highest proportion of sp2 domains. Besides, the lowest proportion of CO bonds of SE-PDA-CDs is also observed from the deconvoluted C 1s spectra. The significantly decreased carbonyl and carboxyl contents in PDA-CDs also contributes to improving their fluorescence performance because the electron-accepting groups can serve as fluorescence quenchers and induce the non-radiative recombination process [48]. Collectively, the XPS analysis results suggest that SE-PDA-CDs are composed of multilayer graphene-like structures containing N-doped sp2 clusters dispersed within an amorphous sp3 carbon matrix, which are hybridized and covalently bonded with some functional groups such as carbonyl and carboxyl groups (Fig. 1).

To assess the purity of SE-PDA-CDs after rigorous dialysis purification, we performed a comparative analysis of the physicochemical properties of the purified CDs obtained using dialysis treatment with different MWCO membranes. The fluorescence spectra of the unpurified supernatant and retentates derived from SE-mediated carbonated PDA solvothermal samples were recorded after dialysis using 1, 5, and 15 kDa MWCO membranes for 7 d. The yields and QYs of these samples were also measured. As the purification level increased, the yield and QYs of the PDA-derived solvothermal products decreased gradually (Fig. 4 a). Concurrently, varying degrees of excitation-dependence emission behaviors were also observed, which diminished as the MWCO value increased (Fig. 4c–f). Recently, emerging evidence has increasingly indicated that the excitation-dependence emission characteristics and high QYs of specific CDs are predominantly attributed to a variety of small-molecule fluorophores and their assembled supramolecular clusters [19,22]. Similarly, our findings from the studies on the fluorescence properties of the 1, 1–5, and 5–15 kDa dialysates, which were obtained through sequential re-dialysis of the 15 kDa dialysate based on molecular weight intervals, showed approaching or comparable QY values (Fig. 4b) and similar degrees of excitation-dependence (Fig. S6) as those observed in the unpurified sample (QY = 8.1 %). Thus, it can be inferred that the commonly used membrane dialysis with low MWCO is inadequate in completely eliminating the fluorescent byproducts associated with SE-PDA-CDs, resulting in incomplete purification. The coexistence of diverse species and structures of small molecule fluorescent byproducts with CDs forms a complex hierarchical system, wherein distinct excited photons correspond to transitions between different energy levels. Consequently, this can result in pronounced excitation-dependent phenomena and an overestimation of strong fluorescence performance. By contrast, the 15 kDa retentate sample exhibits nearly excitation-independent emission (Fig. 4f), preliminarily indicating efficient removal of fluorescent byproducts and high purity of SE-PDA-CDs. The slight emission wavelength variation caused by different excitation wavelengths might be attributed to the heterogeneity in the size and component of the CDs.

Further evidence of the abundant generation of fluorescent byproducts can be provided by UV–vis absorption spectroscopic analysis (Fig. 5 a). In the absorption spectrum of the unpurified CDs solution, a strong absorbance peak at approximately 225 nm was observed. After 1 or 5 kDa membrane dialysis, the absorption spectra of the purified CDs solutions exhibited similar characteristic absorption peaks. Furthermore, the characteristic absorption peak at 225 nm was still observable in the absorption spectra of the 1, 1–5, and 5–15 kDa dialysates (Fig. S7). Unexpectedly, the absorption spectrum of the 15 kDa retentates displayed a broad monotonous absorption, differing from those observed in the aforementioned other CD solutions, suggesting the effective elimination of fluorescent byproducts with characteristic absorption.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was utilized to characterize surface functional groups of various purified CDs samples (Fig. 5b). Compared with the unpurified sample, the FTIR spectra of the dialyzed PDA-derived CDs samples exhibited the absence of an absorption peak at 1349 cm−1, corresponding to CH2 wagging vibrations, along with the emergence of a new absorption band at 3125 cm−1 associated with aromatic C–H and N–H stretching vibrations. Moreover, in contrast to the 1 and 5 kDa retentates, the 15 kDa retentate displayed a notably weakened characteristic absorption band at 3460 cm−1, indicative of –OH stretching vibrations. Given the close correlation between the absorption peaks of CH2 and hydroxyl groups with DA and its derivatives, these findings further confirm that a more rigorous purification protocol can effectively remove undesired fluorescent small molecule byproducts. Raman technique was further used to explore the possible component differences of the CDs samples dialyzed with different MWCO membranes. As shown in Fig. 5c, Raman spectra of all the CDs samples exhibit two peaks at around 1340 and 1570 cm−1, corresponding to disordered (D band) and graphite (G band) carbon structures, indicating the co-existence of sp3-and sp2-hybridized carbon in SE-PDA-CDs. It is worth noting that the Raman spectrum of the unpurified CDs sample has a wide peak width and strong spiking phenomenon, which may be attributed to the reason that the different components in the mixture possess distinct vibration patterns and molecular interactions. As the MWCO value increased, both the peak intensity and peak width of the Raman spectrum for the purified CDs samples gradually decreased, while the spiking signal also weakened. Notably, the 15 kDa retentate exhibited a significant reduction in half-peak width, and the baseline noise was completely eliminated. In contrast, the Raman spectrum of the 15 kDa dialysate had a wide peak width and significant noise, with no observable D band or G band signals. Overall, the Raman results are consistent with the findings of UV–vis and FTIR analysis, verifying that rigorous dialysis treatment can effectively enhance the purity of SE-PDA-CDs.

Next, the CE and NMR techniques, which have been demonstrated to be the powerful tools for high-efficiency fractionating and revealing the potential molecular species in CDs samples [23,26], were utilized to validate the effective purification of SE-PDA-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 5d, the CDs samples with different purification levels were analyzed by CE under optimized conditions. Two chromatographic peaks with different UV absorption characteristics were observed in the electropherograms. Peak 1, which exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 225 nm, was attributed to low-molecular-weight byproducts. Peak 2, which displayed a broad monotonic UV absorption profile, can be attributed to the target CDs product. As the MWCO value increased, the intensity of peak 1 gradually decreased, while the response of peak 2 remained constant. Notably, only peak 2 was observed in the electropherogram of the 15 kDa retentate sample, with peak 1 completely absent. In contrast, only peak 1 was detected in the electropherogram of 15 kDa dialysate. Therefore, the CE separation experiment further confirms the presence of numerous small-molecule fluorophores in the crude product of the CDs supernatant, which can be effectively eliminated through rigorous purification involving 15 kDa membrane dialysis.

In addition to chromatographic techniques, NMR has also been proven as an essential and highly sensitive technique to identify residual molecular species in CDs samples. Due to their complex and highly heterogeneous nature, highly purified CDs samples should not exhibit sharp and resolved signals in their 1H NMR spectra, which serves as an important distinguishing feature from the molecular species that typically possess intense sharp signals [26]. However, the 1H NMR spectrum of the unpurified CDs sample displays intense sharp signals in both the aliphatic region (1–4 ppm) and aromatic region (7–8.5 ppm) (Fig. 5e), indicating the presence of massive residual molecular species. Similar sharp signals were also observed in the 1H NMR spectra of the 1 kDa and 5 kDa retentates, although the intensities of these signals were somewhat reduced. Remarkably, these sharp signals are almost completely eliminated in the 1H NMR spectrum of the 15 kDa retentate, with only broad and non-resolved peaks attributed to CDs being observable. In comparison, the 1H NMR spectrum of the 15 kDa dialysate exhibits numerous sharp signals similar to the unpurified sample. Collectively, the results of the 1H NMR analysis are consistent with the findings of other characterization methods, verifying the effectiveness and necessity of using 15 kDa membrane dialysis treatment in obtaining highly purified SE-PDA-CDs samples.

In addition to the characterization methods mentioned above, MS has also been demonstrated as an effective means to distinguish CDs from small molecular organic byproducts [49]. As depicted in Fig. S8, the molecular weights of graphene quantum dots with diameters of 2 nm and 1.7 nm are simulated as around 1.6 kDa and 1.0 kDa, respectively. Analysis using quadrupole time-of-flight (Q-TOF) MS revealed that the majority of molecular ion peaks from unpurified SE-PDA-CDs samples had mass-to-charge ratios below 609 (Fig. S9). In contrast, a high abundance of ions was found in the mass-to-charge ratio (m/z) range between 1000 and 1500 in the 15 kDa retentate sample. Consistently, matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization (MALDI)-TOF MS analysis of the 15 kDa retentate indicated a predominant m/z distribution from 1 to 1.5 kDa (Fig. S10), accompanied by higher m/z signals around 2.5–4.1 kDa attributed to multi-layer structures. Therefore, the MS results not only align well with the theoretical molecular weight estimation of CDs but also demonstrate that high molecular weight membrane dialysis treatment can effectively remove low molecular weight organic byproducts.

Fig. S11 displays representative TEM images of the 10 and 15 kDa dialysates and retentates of SE-PDA-CDs products. In addition to small amounts of CDs, a significant quantity of undesired amorphous aggregates of byproducts can be clearly observed in the TEM images of the 10 and 15 kDa dialysate fractions. Despite the removal of a large portion of byproducts, the amorphous deposits of undesired organic byproducts persist in the TEM images of the 10 kDa retentate. Consistently, a few intense sharp signals can still be detected in the 1H NMR spectrum of the 10 kDa retentate (Fig. S12). In contrast, no impurities with non-CDs characteristics can be observed in the TEM images of the 15 kDa retentate. Therefore, despite an inevitable loss of some CD products due to the wide gap between the high MWCO and the relatively low molecular weight of SE-PDA-CDs, the efficiency of removing byproducts through high molecular weight (15 kDa) membrane dialysis is significantly enhanced. This process enables the complete removal of organic residues and the attainment of highly purified SE-PDA-CDs.

Based on the aforementioned findings, the high-yield formation of SE-PDA-CDs with high purity and favorable fluorescence properties could be elucidated. Through a solvothermal treatment of the DA precursor mediated by SP/EtOH, efficient and controlled generation of self-polymerization-driven PDA-CDs with uniform sizes is readily achieved. The resulting CDs with an average diameter of 1.8 nm are composed of isolated sp2 domains within multi-layered nitrogen-doped sp3 carbon networks. Remarkably, even after effectively removing low-molecular-weight fluorescent impurities through 15 kDa membrane dialysis, the SE-PDA-CDs are still able to maintain good fluorescence performance, indicating that their blue fluorescence does not arise from small-molecule fluorophores. Furthermore, the as-prepared SE-PDA-CDs exhibit exceptional fluorescence stability under extreme conditions such as exposure to strong acids, strong bases, high salt concentrations, prolonged UV radiation and reduction with sodium borohydride (Fig. S13). These phenomena suggest that the production of fluorescence is not significantly influenced by potential fluorophores adsorbed on the surface of the CDs or surface-state emissions [39]. The blue fluorescence observed in the high-purity SE-PDA-CDs can be mainly attributed to the recombination of electron-hole pairs localized within embedded small sp2 clusters, isolated by the sp3 matrix [46]. Their moderate fluorescence intensity may be due to the limited proportion of localized sp2-domain luminescence centers compared to nonradiative recombination sites, as evidenced by the relatively high ID/IG value of 1.14 revealed by Raman analysis.

To demonstrate the universality of the self-polymerization driven bottom-up synthesis strategy in producing high-purity CDs, we employed a similar approach to prepare fluorescent CDs using commonly available self-polymerizable precursors. Through a solvothermal treatment mediated by SP/EtOH, all the phenolic compounds, including l-dopa, gallic acid, catechol, and pyrogallol, were efficiently transformed into high-purity fluorescent CDs with distinct QYs, with a yield of no less than 15 % for the 15 kDa retentates (Table S6). Similar to the high-purity SE-PDA-CDs, all the self-polymerization driven CDs had good excitation-independent characteristics. Their typical fluorescence emission spectra have been summarized in Fig. S14.

Motivated by the high purity and excellent stability of SE-PDA-CDs, we explored their potential application in sensing field, which heavily relies on the fluorescent properties of CDs. Three types of common targets, including metal cations, anions and antibiotics, were chosen as model analytes to explore the possible impact of purification level on the sensing performance of SE-PDA-CDs (Fig. S15). Tetracycline (TC) was found to exhibit the most pronounced quenching effect on unpurified SE-PDA-CD solutions, which can be attributed to the inner filter effect as indicated by fluorescence lifetime testing and UV–vis absorption spectroscopy analysis (Fig. S16). In addition, several other analytes such as oxytetracycline (OTC), Fe3+, Cu2+, and Pb2+ demonstrated diverse degrees of quenching on the nanoprobes. As the MWCO value increased from 1 kDa to 15 kDa, the interference of other analytes on the TC-responding behavior of the retentates gradually weakened (Fig. 6 a–d). Notably, only TC displayed a significant fluorescence quenching effect on the 15 kDa retentate of SE-PDA-CDs (Fig. 6d), while the fluorescence intensity remained minimally affected by coexisting analytes (Fig. S17a). Besides exhibiting excellent sensing specificity and anti-interference ability, the high-purity SE-PDA-CDs showed ultrafast fluorescence response towards TC, reaching quenching equilibrium within a few seconds (Fig. S17b). On the other hand, owing to interference from molecular byproducts, the unpurified SE-PDA-CDs yielded large measurement errors and relatively poor linearity (R2 = 0.9747) in the quantitative detection of TC. In contrast, the linearity of SE-PDA-CDs for TC sensing progressively improved with increasing purity of the CDs (Fig. 6e). Particularly, the fluorescent quenching value (ΔF) of the 15 kDa retentate showed a highly linear correlation (R2 = 0.9974) with TC content (Figs. S18a and b) and the detection limit was calculated as 60 nM by utilizing the 3σ/slope formula. As outlined in Table S7, the developed SE-PDA-CDs-based probe exhibited greater sensitivity and faster response time for TC detection in comparison to several recently reported CDs-based fluorescent nanoprobes. Based on the significantly improved sensing performance of high-purity SE-PDA-CDs for TC detection, we further evaluated their quantitative detection capabilities in real samples using the standard addition method, including tap water, milk and honey samples. As shown in Fig. S18c, the common coexisting substances of sugars and amino acids had minimal impact on the quenching effect of TC. Furthermore, no TC was detected in the unspiked samples, and the spiked recoveries of TC in these samples ranged from 97.1 % to 105.8 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD, n = 3) lower than 1.34 % (Table S8). Therefore, the above findings highlight the accuracy and practicality of the high-purity SE-PDA-CDs for rapid TC-specific detection in real samples.

Besides fluorescence sensing, CDs have shown great potential in the field of chromatographic separations such as high-performance liquid chromatography and gas chromatography [50,51], due to their rich surface functional groups, small particle size, and moderate adsorption properties. However, the possible significance of the purity of the CDs-based stationary phase materials on their chromatographic separation performance has been widely overlooked in previous studies. In this context, we immobilized unpurified and 15 kDa retentate of SE-PDA-CDs inside capillary as the novel stationary phases for open-tubular capillary electrochromatography (OT-CEC). The successful immobilization of the two CDs samples on the capillary inner wall was validated through the inverted fluorescence microscope and electroosmotic flow (EOF) characterizations, which is attributed to the presence of varying degrees of uniform green fluorescence on the capillary inner surface (Fig. S19) and significantly reduced EOF mobilities (Fig. 6f). By utilizing nucleosides and alkaloids as model analytes, we conducted a comprehensive investigation on the influences of CDs purification levels on their CEC separation performance. The electropherograms illustrating the separation of these analytes on unpurified CDs and 15 kDa retentate-modified columns are presented in Fig. 6g and h, while their corresponding theoretical plates and resolution are summarized in Table S9. The results revealed that baseline separation of both types of compounds could be achieved with high theoretical plate numbers (up to 1.0 × 105 plates/m) after optimizing the CEC conditions. In contrast, the separation efficiencies of the model analytes on the bare and unpurified CDs-modified columns were significantly lower than those of high purity CDs-modified column under the same electrophoretic conditions, highlighting the crucial role of enhancing the purity of CDs for improving their electrochromatographic separation performance.

In conclusion, a facile, efficient, and versatile self-polymerization driven bottom-up growth method has been developed for high yield preparation of high-purity fluorescent CDs. Through rational regulation of the mixed-solvent-mediated polymerization pathway of the model self-polymerizable DA precursor, spontaneous bottom-up polymerization of DA, highly controllable growth of PDA-NPs, sp2 carbon hybridized solvothermal carbonization and high-conversion production of PDA-CDs can all be easily achieved in a one-pot solvothermal reaction. Through high molecular weight membrane dialysis treatment and high-resolution characterization techniques, highly purified PDA-CDs with high yield and good fluorescent performance can be conveniently obtained, and their high purity is thoroughly confirmed. Further studies reveal that the recombination of electron-hole pairs originating from embedded small sp2 clusters within the hybridized carbon networks may be the main cause of the blue fluorescence observed in these high-purity graphitized CDs, providing insights for elucidating the fluorescence mechanism of CDs. Furthermore, the obtained high-purity CDs demonstrate significant importance in enhancing sensing selectivity and improving the efficiency of electrochromatographic separation, which could be employed in the fields of precise detection and advanced separation media fabrication. More importantly, this study presents a comprehensive solution that offers broad applicability for achieving high-yield synthesis, efficient purification, and high-resolution characterization of high-purity CDs, thereby paving the way for the evolution of next-generation CDs in diverse advanced applications.

Long Yuan: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Software, Methodology, Investigation. Meiting Yan: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Xueping Tao: Investigation. Xiao Yang: Validation, Software. Wenwen Long: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Wenli Xia: Investigation. Hongyan Liao: Investigation. Yuanmin Gong: Visualization, Validation. Kailian Zhang: Supervision, Resources. Zhining Xia: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Qifeng Fu: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.
